title: Data Management
text:
The PolliCam pushes all captured images and JSON metadata sidecars to a central storage cluster.


Data is synced via WebDAV. You can retrieve it using standard tools like curl, wget, or dedicated WebDAV clients.


The server organizes data by camera identity and session ID:
/pollicam_data/
├── PolliCam_01/
│   ├── session_20260618/
│   │   ├── session_20260618_120500.jpg
│   │   └── session_metadata.json
├── PolliCam_02/
...



To download the metadata for analysis, you can use rsync or download directly via your browser if the WebDAV server allows it.
# Example: downloading a specific session's metadata
curl -u username:password -O https://storage.cesnet.cz/pollicam/PolliCam_01/session_20260618/session_metadata.json




The metadata sidecar contains summary statistics for each session:
{
  "session_id": "session_20260618",
  "frames_processed": 1420,
  "total_detections": 35,
  "classes_found": {
    "Apis_mellifera": 30,
    "Bombus_spp": 5
  }
}
You can load these JSON files directly into R or Python for downstream ecological analysis.

title: Data Management
PolliCam Field Infrastructure
text:
This is the comprehensive documentation for the YOLO Camera project (PolliCam). Here you will find everything needed to assemble, deploy, and manage the field cameras.



Hardware & Models
SD Card Preparation
Operation & Deployment
Data Management

title: PolliCam Field Infrastructure
text:
Hardware & Models
SD Card Preparation
Operation & Deployment
Data Management

title: Operation & Deployment
text:
The primary software running the camera is the PolliCam Field Daemon.

Continuous Capture & Inference: The daemon continuously captures images and runs YOLO inference locally.
Logging: A metadata sidecar (JSON format) is generated per session, logging detection counts and classes (e.g., Apis_mellifera).
Syncing: The daemon runs a background thread (sync_worker) that periodically attempts to upload data to the CESNET WebDAV server when an internet connection is available.





Mounting: Ensure the 3D-printed enclosure is securely mounted at the observation site.
Power: Connect the power source (battery pack or solar controller) to the Pi.
Boot Sequence: Wait approximately 60 seconds for the Pi to boot and the daemon to initialize.
Network: If using a mobile hotspot for real-time syncing, ensure the hotspot is active. The daemon will automatically detect the connection and begin uploading data. If no connection is found, it will log locally and upload later.
Validation: Connect to the same network as the camera and verify SSH access to ensure it is running without errors.

# Check daemon logs
journalctl -u pollicam -f

title: Fleet Management
text:
As your PolliCam deployment grows, it’s crucial to curate and track your cameras. The system uses a centralized JSON registry to identify cameras, and this page serves as your human-readable inventory.


The PolliCam software automatically identifies which camera it is running on by reading the Raspberry Pi’s CPU hardware serial number. It looks up this serial number in the software/fleet_registry.json file.
Example fleet_registry.json:
{
  "000000001a2b3c4d": "PolliCam-01",
  "00000000deadbeef": "PolliCam-02"
}
Whenever you prepare a new SD card for a new Raspberry Pi: 1. Run cat /sys/firmware/devicetree/base/serial-number on the Pi to get its serial. 2. Add that serial and your chosen name to fleet_registry.json.



Use this table to curate your cameras, their hostnames (for connecting via your phone), and their hardware IDs.










Camera Name
Hostname / Address
Passwords
Hardware Serial
Deployment Site




PolliCam-01
pollicam-01.local
pollicam
000000001a2b3c4d
Site A (Meadow)


PolliCam-02
pollicam-02.local
pollicam
00000000deadbeef
Site B (Forest Edge)


PolliCam-03
pollicam-03.local
pollicam
TBD
Unassigned




Tip: You can access any camera’s control panel from your phone by typing http://<hostname>:5000 in your web browser, as long as it’s connected to your mobile hotspot!

title: SD Card Preparation
text:
Before deploying the PolliCam, the Raspberry Pi SD card must be flashed and configured with the core daemon software.



Download the Raspberry Pi Imager.
Select Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit).
Choose your target SD card.
Click the gear icon to pre-configure:

Hostname (e.g., pollicam-01)
Enable SSH (Use public-key authentication if possible)
Set up your Wi-Fi credentials (for your mobile hotspot)
Set the locale settings.

Write and verify the image.


Tip for Field Access: Because you set a unique Hostname (like pollicam-01), you don’t need to hunt for its IP address on your phone. You can connect using a stable address based on its ID! Just SSH into <hostname>.local (for example, ssh pi@pollicam-01.local).




Once booted, SSH into the Pi using the hostname you configured in the previous step.
# Connect to the Raspberry Pi
ssh pi@pollicam-01.local
Then, simply run this single installation command to automatically set up the camera, install all dependencies, and configure it to boot automatically:
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jakubstenc/YOLO_camera/master/install.sh | bash
Once the installation finishes, the web interface will run automatically as a background service (pollicam-ui.service).
You can access the camera interface from your phone or computer at: https://<hostname>.local:5000

Warning: Because the camera generates a self-signed security certificate to allow GPS access, your browser will show a “Your connection is not private” warning. This is expected! Simply click Advanced and then Proceed to [hostname] (unsafe).




When deploying the camera in the field, you have two options for connecting your phone to the camera’s web interface.


By default, the camera acts as a client and connects to your phone’s mobile hotspot. This gives the camera internet access to upload data automatically. * iOS/Windows: Connect your camera to your phone’s hotspot and simply go to https://<hostname>.local:5000 * Android Devices: Android blocks .local names on hotspots. To fix this, you can run a script that permanently assigns a predictable IP based on the camera’s number (e.g., pi1 becomes ...101): bash     curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jakubstenc/YOLO_camera/master/lock_ip.sh | bash



If your phone has routing issues or you don’t want to use your phone’s battery to run a hotspot, you can convert the camera into its own standalone Wi-Fi hotspot. * Pro: 100% reliable connection (https://192.168.4.1:5000), bypasses all Android bugs. * Con: The camera will not have internet access to auto-upload data. You must download data via SD card or manually connect it to Wi-Fi later.
To enable Standalone Access Point mode, run:
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jakubstenc/YOLO_camera/master/enable_hotspot.sh | bash
Once it finishes, your SSH connection will drop. On your phone, turn off your mobile hotspot, turn ON Wi-Fi, and connect to the new PolliCam-<number> network (it is an Open network, no password required).
